title: 13 Tir — The Miracle at Cana of Galilee & The Assembly
narrative:
The thirteenth day of Tir celebrates the Miracle at Cana of Galilee — the first of the signs by which, as the Evangelist John declares, the Lord manifested His glory, and His disciples believed in Him. The wedding at Cana, which the ancient Alexandrian and Ethiopian traditions number among the three great epiphanies of the Lord's life alongside His Baptism and the visit of the Magi, is the first public manifestation of the divine power within the context of human joy. The Lord was present at a wedding feast — and the fathers have delighted in this: not in a temple, not at a sacrifice, not in a place of formal religion, but at a table of celebration, in the company of ordinary people at the ordinary human event of marriage. His presence there, and the transformation of water into wine at the request of His mother, declares that the Incarnation has consecrated the whole of human life — its feasting as well as its fasting, its joy as well as its repentance.

The miracle of Cana is the first manifestation of glory in the sequence of epiphanic acts that follows the Baptism at the Jordan; the two events together frame the opening of the Lord's public ministry. At the Jordan, He was manifested to John and to those who stood by as the Son of God; at Cana, He is manifested to His disciples as the Lord of creation, the one who has authority over the elements themselves. The water that is transformed into wine — and into the best wine, the wine kept until the end — is a sign that points beyond itself: to the new wine of the Kingdom that the Lord will pour out at the Last Supper, to the wine of the Eucharist, to the marriage feast of the Lamb at the end of all things. Mary's role at Cana — her quiet intercession, 'They have no wine,' and her direction to the servants, 'Whatever He says to you, do it' — makes her the paradigmatic intercessor and the mother of the Church's eucharistic obedience.

Saint Basalide, the soldier-martyr whose monthly commemoration falls on this day, and the holy Martyrs Hermylus and Stratonicus complete this day's assembly. Hermylus was a deacon in Singidunum on the Danube who was arrested during the persecution of Licinius; Stratonicus was his jailer who, witnessing Hermylus's courage and the divine protection that attended his torments, confessed Christ on the spot and was immediately imprisoned alongside the man he had been guarding. Together they were thrown into the Danube River and drowned — a martyrdom by water that the fathers read as itself a form of baptism, a death in the river that mirrors the Lord's death-in-water at the Jordan and His raising of the water to the dignity of the baptismal font.

The theological seal of this thirteenth day of Tir is the mystery of the First Manifestation of Glory. At Cana, the hidden glory of the Incarnate Word breaks through the surface of ordinary life for the first time in the public ministry, just as it broke through the surface of the Jordan at the Baptism. Basalide witnesses to that glory in his martyrdom; Hermylus and Stratonicus witness to it by their death in the river. In each case the manifestation happens unexpectedly, at an unlikely moment and in an unlikely place — a wedding feast, a riverside, a prison — because the glory of the incarnate Lord refuses to be confined to sacred spaces and holy hours. It breaks out wherever love is present, wherever obedience is practiced, wherever two witnesses are thrown into the river together in the name of the Lord who first hallowed the river by His own descent.
